items.correct: The stiffness of a material — the ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region.
items.misconception: Young's modulus measures how strong a material is — how much force it can take before breaking.
items.random_wrong: Young's modulus measures the density of a material.
items.why: Students conflate stiffness (resistance to deformation) with strength (resistance to failure) because both feel like 'tough'.

items.q: What is the formula for Young's modulus?
items.correct: Stress / strain — the ratio in the linear (elastic) region of a stress-strain graph.
items.misconception: Force / area — the same as stress itself.
items.random_wrong: Extension / original length — the same as strain.
items.why: Students remember the component words (stress, strain) but mix up that Young's modulus is their ratio, not one of them alone.

items.q: What happens to a metal wire stretched beyond its elastic limit?
items.correct: It deforms plastically and will not return to its original length when the load is removed.
items.misconception: It snaps immediately at the elastic limit.
items.random_wrong: It becomes more elastic and can stretch further.
items.why: Students treat the elastic limit as the breaking point, conflating it with the ultimate tensile strength or fracture point.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: Correct answer: 5 km. “7 km” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: Correct answer: Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
